title: Anthropological Theory
description: Professor Joanne Rappaport M 5:15 pm â 7:05 pm ICC 210A This course will survey the major debates in sociocultural anthropology that have taken place over the past 75 years. Instead of reviewing the major texts in chronological order, the course will be organized around four anchor texts that are relatively recent, but will allow us to project both backward and forward in time to related debates and issues.

title: Anthropological Theory
description: Professor Joanne Rappaport M 5:15 pm â 7:05 pm ICC 210A This course will survey the major debates in sociocultural anthropology that have taken place over the past 75 years. Instead of reviewing the major texts in chronological order, the course will be organized around four anchor texts that are relatively recent, but will allow us to project both backward and forward in time to related debates and issues.

title: Politics of N. African Masculinities
description: This interdisciplinary course is geared towards an examination of the politics of literary, scholarly, filmic, and journalistic representations of the young North African Muslim man, especially in the shadow of the alarm, fear and anxiety that his presence seems to produce in Euro-American Civilization. Our analytic point of departure will be an examination of the discourses surrounding the male Muslim subject within Western popular media. We will begin by looking at a series of high-profile media cases in which North African masculinity is âon trial.â Early sessions in the semester will explore major scholarly contributions to the field of masculinity studies, prompting us to respond to the following questions: what is masculinity? What is the value of its study? What are the major concerns and preoccupations of scholars in this field of study? How does the scholarly field of masculinity studies provide us with tools to better interpret our chosen real-life cases? Subsequently, we will turn to examine a variety of textual and film sources that have been produced by, about, or, for young North African Muslim men. We will make use of a range of interdisciplinary material on the worlds of men as practiced and imagined in the countries of Libya, Tunisia, Algeria and Morocco, their diasporas, as well as Egypt. We will be especially attentive to the ways in which North African writers and filmmakers have grappled with the question of manhood in specific socio-cultural contexts, as well as the questions, issues, anxieties, and fear that undergird their projects in masculine representation.v

title: Introduction to the Arab World: Theories and Methods
description: An introduction to the theoretical paradigms and the methodologies most appropriate to the study of the Arab World. Covers a spectrum of approaches from Islamic textual analysis and philologically based orientalism through liberal, radical paradigms to modern textual and discourse analysis.

title: Introduction to the Arab World: Theories and Methods
description: An introduction to the theoretical paradigms and the methodologies most appropriate to the study of the Arab World. Covers a spectrum of approaches from Islamic textual analysis and philologically based orientalism through liberal, radical paradigms to modern textual and discourse analysis.

title: Political Identity and Literature in the Arab World
description: It is not a coincidence that the Prophet of the Arabs was accused of being a poet, and their most celebrated poet was accused of claiming to be a Prophet and that both were very much involved in politics. From the time when the poem was the principle means of creating the Arab tribes in the imagination of their members and up until the twentieth century Arab nationalist movements, literature has been of utmost political significance in the Arab world. The axiom that a statementâs beauty was the sign of its truth, applied to religious and secular texts alike, and made the literary value of any discourse an indispensable asset for its political potency. Whether in imagining and thereby creating the self, or in asserting it against colonial negation, or in rebelling, questioning and at times even deconstructing it, Arabic literature has been arguably inseparable from the political history and identity of the Arabs. literature is also the work of individual authors who come from different backgrounds and have varying perceptions of themselves, as individuals and as a collective, this might allow us to approach the question of political identity in the Arab world with a greater degree of complexity and nuance. Connected to, but different from, mere political discourse, literature lies in the space between the personal and the public, the fringe and the mainstream, the platform of the spokesman and that of the rebel. After a brief introduction to examples from the classics of poetry and narrative in Arabic literature as a point of reference and departure, this Seminar will try to approach political identity in the Arab world through the works of a number of prominent contemporary poets and writers, with special focus on the Levant, Iraq, and Egypt. Requirements The course will be taught in standard Arabic, all of the primary sources and literary works are in standard Arabic with very few exceptions in the Egyptian or Levantine dialects. Students should be fluent in the language and have the ability to understand classical and modern literary texts. Students will be required to write two essays, around 3000 words each, the first essay is an analytical review of a literary work, while the second is an essay on one or more of the political themes discussed in class, as reflected in literature. These essays should not be summaries of the works discussed, essays without a theoretical arguments are discouraged. Also, students will be required to write a response paragraph every week on the specified readings. The paragraph should not be a summary of the readings, but an expression of the studentsâ reaction to them.

title: Human Rights Law in the Middle East
description: The 14-week course will be divided into three sections 1) human rights law in substance and theory; 2) case studies; and 3) practice. It will cover the doctrines that constitute international human rights law, the legal fora in which those laws are enforced and/or adjudicated, the controversies surrounding the application of human rights law in the Middle East, and the politics informing the discourse of human rights in the Middle East. The students are expected to take a very active role in the course by bringing in outside readings, participating in discussions, and preparing presentations for their fellow students.

title: History of Iraq: Ottomans to 2003
description: This graduate seminar examines the history of Iraq from the collapse of the Ottoman Empire to the American invasion of 2003. The course will initially briefly analyze the pre-WWI Islamic period concentrating on issues such as the Sunni-Shiâi division, the concept of khilafa (caliphate), and the impact of foreign invasions and Ottoman rule on Mesopotamian society. The focus will then shift to the creation of Iraq by the British under a League of Nations Mandate, the retention after Iraqâs independenceâwith minor modificationsâof the political, administrative, and economic system introduced by the British, and why this system produced a regime such as that of Saddam Hussein. The seminar will also analyze the situation of women and minorities in Iraqi society, and Iraqâs relations with its neighbors.

title: Refugees & Migrants to and from the Arab World
description: This course will explore in detail four refugee situations in the Arab world in the 20th and 21st century: Palestine, Iraq, Sudan, and Somalia. The beginning of the course will focus on the critical scholarship on refugee movements, development/aid, violence, war, resistance, borders, and citizenship, which will be themes used to examine the four case studies. Based largely in anthropological studies of refugee movements, the course will concern itself with the impact to social and family life, identity, and people's sense of place and nation in the context of war and displacement.

title: Financial Markets of the Arab Gulf
description: The premise of the course is that financial markets and business practices in non-democratic developing countries are fundamentally political. States use the financial and business structures to maintain their power. The course seeks to understand how financial markets and business work in the Arab-Persian Gulf [focusing on Saudi Arabia, Kuwait & Bahrain]. Therefore the course will have a very practical bent to it, going into some detail in the techniques used for financing and investing in the Gulf and the differences with other states. The course may be of interest to students of international business, international finance, government/development policy and Arab affairs. The course will seek to be very practical and draw on real life experience of both the instructor and the students.

title: Politics of Nationalism in the Arab World
description: To many of its students Nationalism is a matter of complexity and controversy, yet to many of its adherents; it is a matter of simplicity and consensus. In the Arab world nationalism is both simpler and more complex than it is in many other parts of the world. An area the overwhelming majority of whose inhabitants speak the same language, have the same religion, and have some sort of an emotional consensus about major political issues, at first glance, the Arab world promises to be an easy place to study Nationalism. Yet the Arab world is divided into territorial nation states, where that Arab overwhelming majority becomes, in some cases, less overwhelming. Those modern territorial nation states come with their own nationalisms. The Arab world is also part of the larger Muslim world; the idea of nationalism itself is hence seen by some as a European import that contradicts the political identity based on belonging to the Muslim Umma. Others do not see any contradiction or exclusiveness between the three; territorial nationalisms, Pan-Arabism, and Islamism. A study of nationalism in the Arab world is therefore a study of colonialism and resistance, dependence and independence, economy and culture, as well as a span of expressive activities and representations from military invasions to literary productions. In this seminar we shall attempt to answer questions like: what were the origins of nationalism in the Arab world? What types and kinds of nationalisms developed in the region and why? How does nationalism relate to Islam, colonialism, Ibn Khaldounâs Assabiyya, modernity or even post modernity? How does the Arab Israeli conflict, as well as the American occupation of Iraq affect territorial and Pan Arab nationalisms in the region? Does nationalism, as an ideology, survive the political parties and institutions that represent it? Does it change? And if it does change, can we still recognize it as a form of nationalism or does it change beyond recognition? After a short introduction to the operative concepts in the study of nationalism in a third world context, this seminar shall deal with the historically indicative cases such as the development of Arab nationalism in the 19th century, the Inter war period and the cold war forms of nationalism, Egyptian and Lebanese territorial nationalisms, the Arab Israeli conflict, Arab Nationalism in Iraq, Islam and neo-territorial nationalisms in the post cold war era.

title: Youth, Popular Culture, and Mass Media:  Representations and Interventions in the Contemporary Middle East
description: This course will introduce students to the popular culture enjoyed by their counterparts throughout the Middle East. Readings will provide theoretical frameworks and ethnographic examples enabling students to comprehend key themes in the production and consumption of Middle Eastern television, music, cinema, dance, art and cyberspace. The course will focus on the interplay of economic, political and symbolic capital at the local, regional and global levels. The readings, lectures, presentations, and discussions will address such topics as: identity formation; youth culture; political resistance; cultural authenticity and tradition; globalization and Western influences; nationalism; social class; gender; politicized religion; the commodification of heritage ; morality debates; political conflict; technological innovations; and the distinctions between, and blurring of, high and low culture. The course will encourage students to comprehend and use anthropological concepts in order to understand Middle Eastern popular culture from the point of view of its producers and consumers, and to trace the flows of cultural influences and political ideologies in the Middle East and beyond. The course will feature films, musical and dance performances, and a visit to an art gallery specializing in art created by Palestinian painters, photographers, and sculptors.

title: Economics of the Middle East
description: This course will offer an overview of the economics of the Arab world. It will cover the countries of the Arab side of the Persian Gulf, the Levant, Egypt and North Africa. It will not cover Iran or Sudan except in reference to their role in the economies of the Arab world. The course will not be structured with given readings for each class, on the other hand the text book and articles provided in class or on blackboard should be read in total by the students as the class advances. A great deal of time will be spent looking at the geographical and natural advantages of each country and how the politics of each is affecting their policy making and their economic relations with the rest of the world. We will attempt to define the various forms of economies in the Arab world and which variables influence their policies. For example, we will try to evaluate the role of oil in the development of the area, whether as a curse or a blessing. Students are expected to have a background in Economics, with Micro and Macro being the minimum pre-requisites for the course.

title: Politics of Saudi Arabia & Gulf
description: The course will focus on the dynamics of tradition and modernization in Saudi Arabia, eastern Arabia, Yemen, and the Gulf. It will address the economics, governments, societies, and politics, as well as external relations and foreign policies of Saudi Arabia, Yemen, and the GCC countries, both between and among themselves and with Iran and Iraq, and vice versa. Through readings, discussions, and lectures the seminar will provide an array of information and insight pertaining to these phenomena not commonly found or readily available either in published sources or other academic curricula.

title: Sudan in Crisis
description: This course will examine the complexity of modern Sudan, the root causes of its conflicts, and the regional context. Participants will analyze the challenges and hard choices facing policy-makers, both in Sudan and in the international community, in particular in peace-making and peace-building; identify lessons to be learned from conflicts and peace-making in Sudan; and consider their wider application. A major objective will be development of analytical and presentational skills. Active participation will be required and students should come to class prepared to discuss the issues listed for that session.

title: Media & Communications in the Arab World
description: Since September 11, 2001, much public and scholarly attention has been drawn towards the operations of Arab media institutions. This course delves into the increasingly influential realm of international broadcasting as it applies to the Middle Eastern media milieu. By engaging primary issues pertaining to the development, structure and impact of contemporary Arab media institutions and their content, the course allows students to explore the theoretical approaches underlying a burgeoning "industry" in the region. Topics include the political and economic structures of regional media, the history of Arab journalism, identity-construction and representation, operations of government broadcasting, the advent of satellite television and new media technology and their ramifications. The course also problematizes such notions as the "Al-Jazeera Effect" and "the Arab street" as well as issues including reporting styles in the Arab press, legislation and "freedom of the press" policies, religious and doctrinal broadcasting, and entertainment/pop culture in the region. Through the evaluation of exemplar case studies, the future of Arab media in light of new technological innovations and the advent of "alternative notions" of information delivery including mobile phone messaging, blogging and other forms of digital communication.

title: Politics of Egypt
description: At least since the tenth century the centrality of Egypt to the Arab and Islamic worlds has been more than geographical. From the era when the formative texts on Islamic jurisprudence were produced to the early industrialization attempts in the nineteenth century and beyond, scholars find it difficult to draw the line between the domestic, the regional and the international in Egyptian Politics. This entanglement became more acute in the 20th century. Our understanding of the rise of Islamism, the Arab Israeli conflict, the outcomes of the Iraqi wars of 1980, 1990 and 2003, the conflict in Sudan and the relative weight of the Gulf states cannot be complete if we overlooked political trends inside Egypt; neither can we understand those trends in isolation from the aforementioned regional events. Egypt itself is a problematic concept, for the idea of Egypt has its own not so straight forward history, from Jabartyâs late Eighteenth century âMisrâ, at times referring only to parts of Cairo, to Lutfi Al-Sayyidâs Pharaonic Egypt, to Nasserâs Arab Egypt, to Stadatâs quest for the magic mix of âauthenticity and modernityâ. The fight over the meaning and identity of Egypt continues to this day among various political actors inside and around the country. This course shall attempt to explore Egypt as a contested space, both as a country and as a concept. We shall be dealing with the various phases Egypt passed through since the Napoleonic Expedition of 1798 up until the current rule of Husni Mubarak, with special focus on the link between the domestic and the regional.

title: Tutorial: Arab Studies
description: MAAS Student only. Students must seek adviser, director and department approval to receive credit for tutorials which range from 1 to 3 credits. Do not pre-register for a tutorial without prior departmental approval. Please see department for additional details.

title: Internship:  Arab Studies
description: MAAS Students only. Students must seek adviser, director and department approval to receive credit for internships. Internships range from 1 to 3 credits per semester. Do not pre-register for a internship without prior departmental approval. Please see department for additional details.

title: Thesis Research:  Arab Studies
description: MAAS Students only. Students must have completed the required MAAS Thesis form by October 1st and received adviser, director and department approval. Do not pre-register for thesis research unless you have been approved by the department. Please see the department for more details.

title: Intensive First Level Modern Standard Arabic I
description: This intensive video-based course focuses on developing proficiency in the standard written Arabic language, as well as formal spoken Arabic. It begins with learning of script and phonology, and works rapidly into a wide range of situation-based texts and topics that build vocabulary, grammar, and general communicative competence.

It begins with learning of script and phonology, and works rapidly into a wide range of situation-based texts and topics that build vocabulary, grammar, and general communicative competence.

title: Intensive Second Level Modern Standard Arabic I
description: This intensive course covers topics and situations relating to contemporary Arabic media, literature and culture. Focus is on acquisition of more complex grammatical structures, expanding vocabulary and discourse skills, and on developing competence in a wide range of communicative situations.

title: Intensive Second Level Modern Standard Arabic II
description: This course is a continuation of ARAB-111-70 It moves from Intermediate low to Intermediate high with more emphasis on accuracy and writing skill. This course is for SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Formal Spoken Arabic I
description: This course is intended to increase spoken Arabic proficiency through focusing on intensive practice of interactive functional skills necessary in communicative situations, such as vocabulary retention and retrieval, listening comprehension, and fundamental conversation strategies. It assumes some knowledge of Arabic script and grammatical structure and is designed to enable nonnative speakers of Arabic to communicate actively and appropriately with educated native speakers on a wide range of topics. Prerequisite: at least one year of Modern Standard Arabic.

title: Formal Spoken Arabic II
description: This course is intended to increase spoken Arabic proficiency through focusing on intensive practice of interactive functional skills necessary in communicative situations, such as vocabulary retention and retrieval, listening comprehension, and fundamental conversation strategies. It assumes some knowledge of Arabic script and grammatical structure and is designed to enable nonnative speakers of Arabic to communicate actively and appropriately with educated native speakers on a wide range of topics. Prerequisite: at least one year of Modern Standard Arabic.

title: Colloquial Iraqi Arabic II
description: This course is intended to build proficiency in the Iraqi dialect, through practice of interactive functional skills such as listening comprehension, conversation strategies (linguistic and cultural), and vocabulary building. It assumes knowledge of Arabic script and Modern Standard Arabic grammatical structure. It is designed to enable students to communicate actively and appropriately on a wide range of topics. Prerequisite: at least one year of intensive Modern Standard Arabic.

title: Third Level MSA: Media I
description: This course focuses on authentic Arabic media, including print media, video, and computer-based materials. Activities include reading/listening for comprehension, and discussion of topics related to current events, politics, economics, society, and culture. Taught in Arabic. Prerequisite: two years of Arabic, or permission of the instructor.

title: Third Level MSA: Media II
description: This course focuses on authentic Arabic media, including print media, video, and computer-based materials. Activities include reading/listening for comprehension, and discussion of topics related to current events, politics, economics, society, and culture. Taught in Arabic. Prerequisite: two years of Arabic, or permission of the instructor.

title: Third Level Modern Standard Arabic Media II
description: ARAB-214-70 seeks to familiarize the student with authentic materials from Arabic print, video, and computer-based media. Activities include reading/listening for comprehension and discussion of topics related to current events in the areas of politics, economics, society, and culture--both regional and international. The course is taught solely in Arabic and includes field trips to the headquarters and newsrooms of papers, television channels, and radio stations within Qatar. Every week classroom assignments will deal with different fields of media, such as front page news, editorials, international and regional coverage, economic analyses and social pieces. Regular course activities include working with video and/or audio news from AlJazeera, BBC-Arabic, Al-Hurra, and other local satellite channels. The students will also be expected to translate news materials from American mainstream media. Also, students will have the opportunity to host and perform in mock news talk shows in Arabic and simulate news broadcasting. This course is for SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Advanced Intensive Arabic I
description: This is an intensive, largely media-based course focusing on developing proficiency in reading and discussion of texts in international relations, history, Islam, and current affairs. In addition to extensive reading, it includes composition exercises, review of Arabic grammar, listening skills, and conversation practice. (This course is for MAAS students only.)

title: Intensive Advanced Arabic I
description: This is an intensive, largely media-based course focusing on developing proficiency in reading and discussion of texts in international relations, history, Islam, and current affairs. In addition to extensive reading, it includes composition exercises, review of Arabic grammar, listening skills, and conversation practice. (This course is for MAAS students only.)

title: Intensive Advanced Arabic I
description: This is an intensive, largely media-based course focusing on developing proficiency in reading and discussion of texts in international relations, history, Islam, and current affairs. In addition to extensive reading, it includes composition exercises, review of Arabic grammar, listening skills, and conversation practice.

title: Intensive Advanced Arabic II
