The real options approach is an extension of option pricing theory for managing real assets. This course is about the real options approach to strategic investments. It aims to develop a real options thinking rather than focusing only on the technical aspects of modeling in a real options framework. In that vein, our objective is to introduce real options as a viable means to evaluation of investment opportunities. Selected case studies and material will ensure an in-depth understanding of various concepts in a realistic setting. MBA second year students only.

title: Operations Management
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core This course is designed to introduce you to concepts, tools and techniques for managing this function. Moreover, since this is a new field for many of you, we also want to give you a new perspective to look at organizations. If we are successful, many of you after this course will change the way you look at a restaurant, a hospital, a bank, a factory, a repair shop, a daily newspaper, an airline, a warehouse, a government agency, an airport, or even a consulting company! Youâll observe more closely and critically how they are producing their product (goods or services), and will notice that some of their production processes are smart and some not-so-smart (sometimes irritatingly inefficient). We believe this course will give you a better appreciation of the real drivers behind success or failure of many of these organizations. To use our usual business school jargons, youâll learn about the strategic importance of the firmâs supply chain and production systems.

title: Project Management
description: The course is open to all MBA students. No pre-requisite. This course provides an overview of project management. The course addresses (1) the culture, (2) the principles, and (3) the basic techniques of project management. The course reviews the general stages of a project in rough chronological order and describes how the stages interrelate. Basic tools of project management, such as work breakdown structure, scheduling, earned value analysis, and risk management, are introduced. The elements of project management critical to the success of a project also are identified and explained. Specific Objectives: 1. To describe, demystify, and formalize project management so that students are aware of the Project Management discipline and can utilize new knowledge and skills in their professions. 2. To enable the immediate practice of project management in any workplace, independent of discipline. 3. To lay a foundation for those students interested in the PMP Certification. 4. To enjoy the class and have spirited, knowledgeable debates with classmates and instructor.

title: Global Logistics
description: The emphasis of the course is the logistics or Supply Chain function in firms that source, produce, distribute and market in multiple nations. The management of the logistics in such firms differs from its domestic counterpart along several key dimensions. First, there is the need to be able to identify and analyze factors that differ across nations, which influence the effectiveness of this function. These include worker productivity, process adaptability, governmental concerns, transportation availability, and culture and so on. In addition, because of the distances involved, transportation and distribution are of greater significance. Finally, these geographically dispersed set of facilities and markets must be integrated and manage to enhance the strategy of the business unit. Therefore, some of the sessions of the course will focus on cross-national decisions and others will focus on managing across nations. Throughout the course we will emphasize the role that emerging technologies are playing within the supply chain and e-commerce. For the purposes of the course, the perspective will be that of the director of logistics or Supply Chain, vice-president of logistics or other, senior level general managers with primary responsibility for the production and distribution of manufactured products. A major theme throughout the course is that sourcing, manufacturing and distribution are of primary importance in the formulation of business and corporate strategy. We want to look at the strategic decisions within logistics as they impact the ability of the firm to achieve its selected overall strategies.

title: Operations Strategy
description: A firm derives a competitive advantage either by achieving a cost advantage, or through product differentiation (Michael Porter, Competitive Advantage, Free Press, NY, NY, 1985). That is, either the firm must be able to produce its goods at a lower cost (through process innovation), or it must offer features that customers desire and competitors lack (through product innovation). We explore specific operational strategies that the firm might pursue to achieve this superior process or product innovation. Within each topic, we develop a framework, or theory, that the firm can use as an aid in its decision-making process, and also tackle a real-life problem through a case study. The topics are as follows: Introduction: The Dynamics of Innovation; Competing Through Operations; Strategic Implications of the Learning Curve; Encroachment Strategies; Outsourcing; New Product Development; Strategic Quality Management; Capacity Management; Capacity, Flexibility and Timing; Path to Profitability; Value Creation in Private Equity.

title: Intensive First Level Persian I
description: This course introduces students to the basic structures of the Persian language. All four language skills: speaking, listening comprehension, reading comprehension, and writing will be taught equally using the immersion method. This method will help students achieve confidence communicating in the Persian language. Aspects of Persian culture will be introduced on a regular basis. At the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. Have basic level conversation in Persian: 1st level 5 minutes, 2nd level 10 minutes. 2. Read texts of elementary level: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 3. Write short paragraphs with reasonable accuracy: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 4. Develop cultural awareness through readings, films, music, etc.

title: Intensive First Level Persian II
description: This course introduces students to the basic structures of the Persian language. All four language skills: speaking, listening comprehension, reading comprehension, and writing will be taught equally using the immersion method. This method will help students achieve confidence communicating in the Persian language. Aspects of Persian culture will be introduced on a regular basis. At the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. Have basic level conversation in Persian: 1st level 5 minutes, 2nd level 10 minutes. 2. Read texts of elementary level: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 3. Write short paragraphs with reasonable accuracy: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 4. Develop cultural awareness through readings, films, music, etc.

title: Intensive Second Level Persian I
description: Permission needed from Instructor. This intensive intermediate level I course is designed to continue developing the student's communicative skills (reading, writing, listening, and speaking). This course is conducted in the target language. Initially, it affords students a view of basic Persian, then an introduction to more complex syntactic grammatical forms. Aspects of Persian history, culture, and contemporary life are also introduced through readings, listening materials, videos and films, and through the use of language technologies (web tools). The students will also be given practice in reading aloud and giving oral presentations of materials taken from the web, media, and television broadcast. As a final project, students will write a composition of 200-350 words (Intermed. I), 400-500 words (Intermed.II).

title: Intensive Second Level Persian II
description: Permission needed from Instructor. This intensive intermediate level I course is designed to continue developing the student's communicative skills (reading, writing, listening, and speaking). This course is conducted in the target language. Initially, it affords students a view of basic Persian, then an introduction to more complex syntactic grammatical forms. Aspects of Persian history, culture, and contemporary life are also introduced through readings, listening materials, videos and films, and through the use of language technologies (web tools). The students will also be given practice in reading aloud and giving oral presentations of materials taken from the web, media, and television broadcast. As a final project, students will write a composition of 200-350 words (Intermed. I), 400-500 words (Intermed.II).

title: Advanced Persian I
description: Two years of Persian and permission of the instructor. This course is designed to enable the student to reach higher levels in the various language skills a stage where they use Persian in wider arrays of cultural, professional and social contexts. Students will prepare newspaper and journal articles, short stories, reports and presentations. Students will be expected to argue and debate extensively, paraphrase and summarize texts, and to express points of view in both speaking and writing. Emphasis will be placed on understanding nuances, idiomatic expressions, and rhetorical devices. By the end of the course, it is expected that students will be able to converse in a clear and concise participatory fashion and to carry out a wide variety of communicative tasks requiring diverse discourse strategies.

title: Advanced Persian II
description: Two years of Persian and permission of the instructor. This course is designed to enable the student to reach higher levels in the various language skills a stage where they use Persian in wider arrays of cultural, professional and social contexts. Students will prepare newspaper and journal articles, short stories, reports and presentations. Students will be expected to argue and debate extensively, paraphrase and summarize texts, and to express points of view in both speaking and writing. Emphasis will be placed on understanding nuances, idiomatic expressions, and rhetorical devices. By the end of the course, it is expected that students will be able to converse in a clear and concise participatory fashion and to carry out a wide variety of communicative tasks requiring diverse discourse strategies.

title: General Pharmacology
description: This course is designed to provide the student with a knowledge of the fundamental principles of pharmacology and a comprehensive experience with the effects of drugs on mammalian organisms. The subject is taught by lectures, conferences, and demonstrations, and is primarily for medical students. Prerequisites: BCHB-501, 502 or equivalent; PBIO-502 or equivalent; ANAT-512 or equivalent.

title: Graduate Pharmacology
description: This course includes both a broad survey of pharmacology and detailed discussions of experimental approaches used in pharmacological research. PBIO501 should be taken concurrently.

title: Advanced General Pharmacology
description: This course consists of an in-depth analysis of selected major units presented in PHAR-504. PHAR504 is a prerequisite.

title: Modern Methods in Molecular Biology
description: Introduction and evaluation of most of the commonly used methods in molecular biology.

title: Applied Statistical Principles in Pharmacology
description: To provide students with a working knowledge of how to design and analyze pharmacological experiments. Course Structure: Each week, students will be given a take-home problem set with a brief (30-min.) introduction to the new topic area. This problem set will be discussed in a âroundtableâ format during the first 90-min. of the following session, and then a new problem set will be introduced for the following week. Students will be evaluated based on their level of participation, homework, and quizzes.

title: Ethical Issues in Scientific Research
description: Discussions of ethical questions and dilemmas facing scientists today.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology
description: This minicourse presents an overview of some aspects of pharmacology. It begins with basic principles of pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics. Neuropharmacology, including the autonomic nervous system and the central nervous system are then covered. Cardiovascular pharmacology and its central control is discussed. Finally, there is a specialized discussion of the role of voltage and ligand-gated channels in controlling cellular function. The course meets from mid-semester to December. Check with bwolfe01@georgetown.edu for exact start date.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Pharmacology I - basic principles
description: Fundamental concepts in pharmacology such as drug-receptor interactions via radioligand binding, functional agonisim, and antagonists as well as drug metablolism and pharmacokinetics will be covered. Meets M-F 10-12 in January. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology II - Drug targets
description: Ion channels, GPCR, S/T kinases and phosphatases, growth factors and receptors, transcription factors will be covered. Additionally cell death by necrosis and apoptosis as well as death and dependence receptors will be presented. Meets 10-12 Jan-Feb. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology III - Neuropharmacology I
description: The autonomic nervous system will be covered. Excitatory neurotransmission in the CNS will be presented including receptors and signal transduction systems for glutamate. LTP, LTD and retrograde signaling will be covered. Meets M-F 10-12 in February. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology IV - Neuropharmacology II
description: Inhibitory neurotransmission by GABA and the many pharmacological tools used to treat anxiety, epilepsy, insomnia, etc. will be presented. Drugs used in the treatment of pain will be covered. Cholinergic and adrenergic neurotransmission and systems in the CNS will be covered. Drugs used to treat scizophrenia and depression will be presented. Meets M-F 10-12 in March. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology V - General Pharmacology
description: Treatment of cancer, viral and bacterial diseases will be covered. Obesity and diabetes as well as lipid metabolism will be presented. Inflammatory diseases and asthma as well as anticoagulants will be covered. Meets M-F 10-12 in April. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology VI - Cardiovascular and GI Pharm
description: Treatment of arrhythmias, heart failure, angina, and hypertension are covered. The enteric nervous system and GI disorders as well as reproduction are presented. Meets M-F 10-12 in April. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Fundamentals of Basic and Clinical Pharmacology

title: DRUG DISCOVERY AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
description: This course is comprised of a series of seminars by specialists in drug discovery and development areas concerning both therapy and diagnostics. It will focus on the scientific, clinical, economic and regulatory processes as well as stewardship involved bringing a drug from the laboratory to the marketplace.

title: Pharmacology Tutorial
description: Individualized tutorials between students and faculty to address topics not covered by other courses.

title: Research
description: Individualized research tutorial with laboratory component. Open only to MS students in the Pharmacology program.

title: Continuing Registration in Pharmacology
description: Continuing Registration is for students who have completed all requirements for the Ph.D. degree but who have not yet graduated. M.S. students can use this mechanism to maintain their student status while finishing needed requirements.

title: Thesis Research--Pharmacology

title: Thesis Research--Pharmacology
description: Thesis research leading to a PhD dissertation. This section 03 is for the student who is still in coursework, but also in thesis research.

title: Introduction to Ethics
description: PHIL 020-Sections 01-11 This course is an introduction to the study of ethics, understood as both a subject for theoretical reflection and a practical guide for how to live. We will explore the views of the Westâs greatest ethical thinkers, both past and present, and we will use narratives from literature and film to illuminate the ethical issues those thinkers raise. Readings will range from Greek tragedy to contemporary articles on sweatshop labor. The course begins with an exploration of the moral self, including conceptions of character, the role of conscience, moral education, moral luck, responsiveness to evil, and the meaning of integrity and self-respect. We will move from there to a discussion of ethical issues that arise out of interactions with others, with a special focus on our immediate Georgetown community. We will discuss the moral ramifications of academic honesty, alcohol use, sexual relationships, and Facebook, using philosophical readings to help us think reflectively about how these issues influence life at Georgetown. Finally, we will broaden our outlook to consider global ethical issues, including our relationship to the world's poor. Requirements include two papers, a take-home midterm, a take-home final, and class participation.

title: Introduction to Ethics: Aristotle & Kant
description: PHIL-010-12 The point of this course is to provide the students with an in-depth introduction to two of the most influential ethical theories in the canon of Western philosophy. The focus will be on the foundations of these theories. We will give the primary ethical texts in both systems a close reading and we will investigate the reasons why the two systems are in such contrast to each other. This is a text oriented course. You will be responsible on the mid-term and the final for the content of all the texts covered in the lectures. Time permitting, the course will close with a consideration of the challenge, if any, posed by evolutionary theory, neuroscience, and related disciplines to the positions defended by Aristotle and Kant. Information Required Texts: Aristotle Introduction to Aristotle (0-07-553652-5) McGraw-Hill Kant Critique of Pure Reason (1-40391-195-9) St. Martinâs Press Grounding for the Metaphysics of Morals (0-915145-00-6) Hackett Critique of Practical Reason (0-02-307753-0) Macmillan Paperbacks Metaphysics of Morals (0-521-56673-8) Cambridge U. Press Office Hours etc.: TBA Jay Reuscher 218 New North reuschej@georgetown.edu reuschej@yahoo.com (H) 202-388-0681 (There is an answering machine.) Any proven violation of the honor code will result in an F for the course.

title: Introduction to Ethics
description: Moral issues such as abortion and euthanasia are plagued by seemingly intractable disagreement. This disagreement makes it difficult to believe that morality is objective in the sense that it applies to all people independently of their culture, feelings, situation, or religion. If morality were objective in this way, it would provide a means for reaching conclusions on these difficult moral issues. This course surveys ethical theories with the goal of determining which (if any) of the theories provide an adequate account of morality. We will assess the viability of ethical theories in two ways. First, we will look at theoretical benefits and problems of the theories. Second, we will examine the extent to which ethical theories yield defensible conclusions on particular moral issues. Students will read philosophical writings on ethical theories and moral issues with an eye toward learning how to understand, present, and critically assess philosophical arguments. Students will be given the opportunity to apply moral theories to real-world cases, and to develop and criticize arguments concerning those cases.

title: Introduction to Ethics
description: Phil 010 (Section 14) Introduction to Ethics In this course, we will explore the foundations of Western ethical thought through close-readings of influential historical texts. Our central question will be: what makes a life a good, or happy, life? In the first part of the course, we will consider whether the good life requires having a good character and organising our entire lives around the right goals, or whether it requires only performing the right action each time. But what makes an action good, or right? Is it the consequences of the action, or our motivation in performing it? In the second part of the course, we will ask under what conditions ethical requirements might be suspended â perhaps because God demands it, or even because of âthe death of Godâ. What could guide our lives or actions in the absence of universal ethical rules? To address these issues, we will read texts by Aristotle, Mill, Kant, Kierkegaard and Nietzsche. We will also see how these various ethical frameworks play out in the context of specific moral dilemmas and concrete issues â such as universal education, capital punishment, lying, and the Leopold and Loeb case. Our goals are: to appreciate the historical development of ethical thought, to understand the different frameworks that underlie ethical thinking and intuitions, to learn to apply these frameworks to particular cases and see how they generate different recommendations, and to critically question and assess different ways of thinking about ethics and the good life.

title: Introduction to Philosophy
description: Philosophy 20, sections 1-10: Intro to Philosophy Prof. W. Blattner What is human freedom?Â Is there such a thing anyhow? Are you free when you choose what to do? Or does freedom require that you choose rationally? Or alternatively, does freedom require that your choices reflect your character, who you are? If freedom does require that you express yourself in your choices and/or actions, are there any moral or aesthetic constraints on such self-expression? Or finally, does freedom require that whatever you choose, you choose in a completely unconstrained manner, free of all determining and limiting factors? We will examine these alternative conceptions of the freedom of action and choice by working through four models of freedom present in the Western philosophical tradition: those of St. Thomas Aquinas, David Hume, William James, and Friedrich Nietzsche. This course will place a premium not just on learning these views, but putting them into debate with one another.Â We will try to sort out our understanding of human freedom and the arguments that can be marshaled for or against these several conceptions of it.Â You will practice the art of philosophical analysis and argumentation in your papers, in which you will learn how to take a philosophically informed stand on the issues and defend it with argument.Â You will also practice this art in weekly mandatory discussion sections. Students will take one short in-section quiz and write three short (5 pp.) papers. Philosophy 20 is part of the Main Campus core curriculum and satisfies the general philosophy half of the philosophy requirement.Â Seniors should not take Philosophy 20, and students who have already taken Intro to Ethics are discouraged from taking Philosophy 20.

title: Intro to Philosophy
description: TBA

title: Introduction to Philosophy
description: Introduction to Philosophy. Spring 2010 Prof. W.Ver Eecke This course will address philosophically the relation of a human being to other human beings. In a first chapter, we will analyze the characteristics of philosophy. We will pay special attention to multi-culturalism as a threat to philosophy in as much as it may lead to skepticism about truth. We will use a book by Jaspers and two videos to provide a summary view of six major civilizations. We will read Plato as an example of philosophical thinking. In a second chapter we will study the interdependence of single humans beings by means of a classic passage in Hegel's philosophy: the master-slave dialectic. We will use Frederick Douglassâ Memoirs as an illustration of Hegelâs ideas. We will discover the paradoxical consequences of dominance/submission and idealization and the positive function of work and thinking. In chapter three we will study the relation of one human being with privileged others. We will do that by first studying the complexity of the human subject (jokes, slips of the tongue, denials) and then by studying the function of privileged others (parents) in the complexity of the human subject. We will make use of philosophy of psychoanalysis which will introduce us to the meaning of both mental illness (schizophrenia and depression) and sexual difference. A member of the counseling center will address the class as part of the Engelhard program. He will concentrate on depression. In a fourth and last chapter we will analyze the relationship of individuals in the capitalist system. We will, first, reflect on the relationship between economic actors in economic theory using the concepts of private, public and merit goods. We will argue that individuals in the free market must compete (private goods), co-operate (public goods) and must submit to the will of the community (merit goods). Second we will study the consequences for a human person of being a member of a special class. Here we will use Marx' analysis of class conflict and class exploitation. Readings: Jaspers. Socrates, Buddha, Confucius, Jesus.Harvest. ISBN 0-15-683580-0. Plato. Great Dialogues of Plato. Mentor. ISBN 0-451-62827-6. Douglass, Frederick. Narrative of the life of Frederick Douglass. On the web: //docsouth.unc.edu/douglass/douglass.html Ver Eecke. Denial, Negation and the Forces of the Negative. SUNY Press.ISBN 0-7914-6599-3 Marx. Communist Manifesto. International Publishers. ISBN 0-7178-0241-8 Some articles will be on reserve and on blackboard

title: Introduction to Philosophy
description: This course is a general introduction to philosophy. The first half of the course will be about moral and general skepticism. Moral skeptics maintain that there are no objective moral truths--no moral facts; there are only feelings and culturally induced habits that prompt you to behave non-selfishly. Or perhaps the only moral fact is that you should behave selfishly? General skeptics question whether you have sufficient grounds for thinking there is even a physical world. You think, you reason, you have plenty of experiences, but are they experiences of anything? Is there any reason to believe reality is as you suppose? The second half of the course is metaphysics. Here we will assume you do have a body, and are not just a disembodied mind. You will be asked to explain what the relation is between your mind and your body, and to figure out whether you act freely sometimes or you are merely a cog in the relentless causal machine that is the universe. In addition to watching some useful films you will read excerpts from the writings of great philosophers, extract key concepts and arguments and then analyze those arguments. Four 3-page papers. Participation in class and Blackboard discussions.

title: Political and Social Thought
description: Prof. Henry S. Richardson Spring 2010: draft Office: New North 205 Email: richardh@georgetown.edu (please use email, not phone) Office hours: tbd Section leaders (sections): email: Office hours (in NN 205): Mark Formicelli (tbd) mtf33@georgetown.edu tbd Kelly Heuer (tbd) kellyheuer@gmail.com tbd Anthony Manela (tbd) aam62@georgetown.edu tbd The ideal of democracy is quite a complex one. As children, we get introduced to it via the idea of majority rule; yet no contemporary government operates on the basis of simple majority rule. The scale of modern governments introduces a need for representative government, which is already a serious complication. Further, the potential for the powerful to dominate, whether absent institutions of majority rule or despite them, must be addressed. Deciding policies or picking rulers by lottery would be fair and impartial in a way, and yet in democracies, we think, citizens should debate policies. Why? Individual rights, as often enshrined in constitutions, do delimit the scope for majority rule as expressed via elected legislatures; but are these rights (such as the rights of free speech and assembly) hindrances to democracy or necessary props of it? Governments also have come to rely on large bureaucracies to work out and implement the policies that legislatures decide upon. Does this reliance destroy the possibility of rule by the people? And what does rule by the people have to do, anyway, with majority rule? In this course, we will read some of the main texts of Western political philosophy. They have been chosen so as to put you in a position, by the end of the semester, to understand democracy in a deeper and more nuanced way. Each of the authors we will read makes an important and distinctive contribution to the democratic ideal. Whether each of their contributions should be incorporated into that ideal is a controversial question; but at least you will be equipped, by the end of the course, to address those questions about the democratic ideal in a thoughtful way. Assigned texts: Machiavelli, The Prince and the Discourses (McGraw-Hill). Hobbes, Leviathan, ed. Curley (Hackett). Locke, Political Writings, ed. D. Wootton (Mentor). Rousseau, The Social Contract and Discourses (BN Publishing) Kant, Perpetual Peace and Other Essays (Hackett) Mill, On Liberty and Other Essays, ed. J. Gray (Oxford). Rawls, Justice as Fairness: A Restatement (Harvard).

title: Ethics:  Catholic Ethics
description: BRADLEY: PHIL-104â01: INTRODUCTION TO CATHOLIC ETHICS Spring 2010 Reason has an indispensable role in figuring out how religious believers as well as non-believers ought to act. Both must pursue, in the earthly city, the genuine human good (Augustine). âCatholic ethics,â as it is the subject matter of this course, refers solely to the philosophical effort, by Roman Catholics, to understand â apart from any appeal to revelation, supernatural faith, or ecclesiastical authority â what is morally good for all men and women whether Christian believers or not. Traditional Catholic philosophical ethics is a ânatural law ethicsâ whose historical sources can be traced to Plato, Aristotle, and the Stoics. In the 13th century, Thomas Aquinas synthesized and clarified earlier tradition by precisely locating the human source of natural law in practical reason. According to Aquinas, when we deliberate about what is morally good and bad, our practical reasoning starts from certain immediately known precepts about what is to be done or not done. These precepts of practical reason are the ânatural lawsâ which universally bind all moral agents. They are the starting points for reasoning about further and more complex ethical issues, whose answers are, accordingly, far from âself-evident.â In modern Roman Catholic thought, a correctly developed philosophical natural law ethics has been regarded as compatible with and even an important source of the Churchâs moral teaching. However, secular philosophers, especially those of a utilitarian bent, widely ignore or reject natural law ethics. Moreover, some present-day Roman Catholic ethicians regard traditional (Catholic) natural law ethics as promising too much and delivering too little. Can we really construct, relying on philosophical reason alone, an ethics that in no significant way deviates from â indeed, gives independent reasons for holding â official Roman Catholic (i.e., ecclesiastically normative) moral teaching? Consider the controversial resolutions offered for such issues as abortion, birth control, in vitro fertilization and transfer of embryos, surrogate motherhood, cloning, non-marital heterosexual or homosexual sexual activity, homosexual marriage, etc. Current discussion of these difficult issues raises many philosophical challenges to traditional Roman Catholic philosophical ethics. These philosophical challenges need to be explored and met on their own territory. What, then, can a contemporary Roman Catholic natural law ethics say in response? In particular, we shall look sympathetically but critically at the philosophical answers of two important Roman Catholic authors, Professors Germain Grisez and John Finnis. title: Ethics: Medieval Homicide
description: Course Description Is it ever morally permissible to execute criminals, to commit suicide, to kill in war? And if it is, under what conditions is it permissible? These and similar questions were the subject of intense discussion in medieval Catholic thinkers, from St. Augustine in the late 4th and 5th centuries, to thinkers such as St. Thomas Aquinas and others in the 13th and 14th centuries and thinkers of the later Counter-Reformation. The theories they developed on these issues have deeply affected the way we think about them now. The distinction between just and unjust wars is a case in point. This course will focus on these medieval discussions of ethical issues pertaining to homicide. In particular, we will consider discussions of the killing of animals versus humans, killing in self-defense, killing in war, the death penalty, suicide, abortion, and contraception. Much of what we read is drawn from the writings of theologians and makes frequent reference to Christian teaching and Scripture. But these theologians were also highly trained in philosophy, and we will be approaching the material from a philosophical rather than a theological perspective. At the end of each section of the course, we will also examine related and often contrasting material in the current philosophical literature.

title: Ethics:  Beginning/Ending Life
description: Itâs a truism that we all die. And that weâve all been born, too. In that sense, the beginnings and endings of human life, it is well known, are inescapable. But even if we assume Bob Dylan was wrong, and that itâs possible for us to be neither busy being born nor busy dying, this wonât be of much help in escaping. For it is precisely in that case that we are most likely to find ourselves in the position of creating life or watching it fade from others we care for. This class is organized around the many moral problems at the bookends of life, which, I conclude, if not constitutive of the entirety of life, are at least in close and constant contact with the rest of it. Whether terminating a pregnancy by removing and discarding a fetus is permissible is a worthy and difficult question, but it is far from the only moral issue haunting the beginning of life. In this portion of the course, weâll start from the very beginning and ask whether conception is itself an unjustifiable act, under certain conditions or under any conditions at all. Weâll confront the puzzles surrounding harm in a context where the imposition of harm determines the identity of those who suffer it, and thus creates none of the usual grounds for complaint, but seems problematic nonetheless. After that but before abortion, weâll also look at the possibility of modifying and enhancing rather than excising an embryo. Difficult as things are at the outset of life, easy conclusions are also not generally available at the other end of life. Weâll begin the course by taking up philosophical issues raised by death. Problems here range from the definition of death, the permissibility of assisted suicide, euthanasia, viability of advance directives in the case of the severely demented, and questions of resource allocation.

title: Ethics:  Foundations of Morality
description: Foundations of Morality If we ask people what we should do about some morally contentious practiceâabortion, for exampleâweâll get a wide range of answers. Some will say that we should ban abortion for the same reason we ban murder: both practices are morally wrong because they show disregard for human life. Others will say that we should permit abortion because it respects womenâs right to choose, and respecting rights is a moral thing to do. But each answer raises a further question. What makes it morally right to respect human life? Why is respecting peopleâs rights a moral thing to do? More generally: what, in the end, makes things morally right or morally wrong? Different philosophers have answered this question differently. Some argue that, at bottom, morality rests on Godâs commands. Others claim that human nature somehow determines morality. Still others suggest that whatâs right and whatâs wrong is a matter ultimately settled by oneâs culture. In this course, we will critically examine these, and other, answers to the question of what, if anything, grounds morality.

title: Ethics:  Autonomy
description: The idea of autonomy is of central importance as an element in our conception of a good life and a just polity, as a condition of moral responsibility and effective consent in medical ethics, and as an aspect of successful human action more generally. We will examine the idea of autonomy, or the family of ideas that go under this label, from all three points of view: from the point of view of normative theory (moral and political), the theory of moral responsibility, and the philosophy of action. In action theory, we will look at ways in which the idea of autonomy, or self-rule, can help elucidate the nature of human choice. In examining the issue of moral responsibility, the question will be whether autonomy is an important condition of such responsibility and, if so, how it should be understood. Finally, we will make a foray into political theory to examine questions raised by feminist thinkers about autonomy and oppression, as well as the ways in which democracies can offer a form of collective self-rule, or autonomy. Examining each of these different guises of autonomy will illuminate all of the others. Crosscutting issues that we will examine include the relation between autonomy and âhigher-orderâ reflection, the relation between autonomy and objectivity, and the question of whether the concept of autonomy stands free of specific normative presuppositions.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: In this course we will examine the formal structure of arguments; we will critically examine the relationship between an argument's premises and its conclusion. Initially we will expose common fallacies of reasoning that you are sure to come across in many contexts of public discourse. Then we will develop a system of formalizing the basic propositional structure of arguments, which you will use to prove that the conclusions of some arguments necessarily follow from their premises. In addition, we will utilize modern methods of semantics to uncover the deeper conceptual relationships that commonly hold among sentences of convincing arguments. You will be able to demonstrate why such arguments convince, and, perhaps, why some initially appealing arguments are nonetheless illogical. In addition to satisfying the philosophy major's requirement in logic, successful completion of this course will lead to better skills in reasoning on any topic.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: Intro to Logic: PHIL-150-01 David Pierce Logic is the study of arguments, more specifically, of what distinguishes sound arguments from fallacious ones. We will study inductive as well as deductive logic, but the bulk of the course concerns formal deductive logic. We begin by studying the main patterns of inductive reasoning: the argument from analogy, Millâs method, and Hypothetical Induction. Then we will study the traditional Aristotelian logic of syllogisms, the Boolean approach characterized by the use of Venn Diagrams, and finally modern symbolic logic which utilizes truth tables and the method of deduction. A high degree of technical or mathematical ability is not required; this is the most elementary course in the logic sequence and does not presuppose any previous knowledge of logic or philosophy. There will be one short paper, three quizzes, and a final exam. Required Readings: Davis. An Introduction to Logic

title: Kant's Aesthetics
description: KANTâS AESTHETICS Spring PHIL-151-01 Jay Reuscher reuschej@georgetown.edu Aesthetics, as a strictly philosophical concern, is very difficult to handle because it occupies a kind of twilight zone between theories of knowledge and theories of nature. On one side, it makes claims that look like knowledge claims because they expect universal agreement even though they cannot be held to rigorous standards of proof. On the other side it makes claims that seem to involve a deep commitment to purposes whose implications are a challenge to philosophical credibility. Moreover, the chief merit of Kantâs approach to this subject is also the source of a major difficulty it presents to the student. He takes many of the toughest problems in aesthetics and integrates them one by one into an already established comprehensive theory of knowledge and nature whose coherence is not beyond controversy. Nevertheless, his approach does provide a sweeping view of how science, morality, and beauty belong together, and it is this view that generously rewards the effort it takes to achieve it. In order to cope with the challenge presented by this material, the course will proceed in the following stages: First, we will take an introductory look at the tensions in Aristotleâs theory of knowledge and nature with its emphasis on purpose to understand one of the main reasons why the Renaissance Platonic revival of which Kant is the 18th century endgame turned away from that style of doing philosophy. Second, we will closely examine the main lines of Kantâs theory of knowledge and his view of the use of purpose in science. Finally, we will do a close reading of the primary texts on the aesthetic judgment and the origins of fine art. There will be exams on each of these stages worth respectively 15 pts., 35 pts., and 50 pts. Required texts: Introduction to Aristotle. McGraw-Hill (0-07-553652-5) Critique of Pure Reason. St. Martinâs Press (1-40391-195-9) Critique of Judgment. Hackett (0-87220-025-6)

title: Stoics and Their Rivals
description: THE STOICS, THEIR HEROS, AND THEIR RIVALS âSome things are in our control and others are not. Things in our control include our opinions, pursuits, desires, and aversionsâ¦ Things not in our control include our health, property, reputation, commandâ¦ The things in our control are by nature free, unrestrained, unhindered, and our own; but those not in our control are weak, slavish, restrained, and belong to others. Remember, then, that if you suppose that what is enslaved is free, and that what belongs to others is your own, then you will be hindered. You will lament, you will be disturbed, and you will curse both gods and men. But if you suppose that only to be your own which is your own, and leave that which belongs to others as it is, then no one will ever compel you or restrain you. You will be free.â These are the opening lines to the Handbook of Epictetus. And we can see in those lines the promise of Stoicism, one of the most significant schools of Western philosophy. That promise lies in total freedom from our captivity, our captivity to our professors and our families, our captivity to the expectations of others, our captivity to the Myth of the Modern World. But that promise comes with a price, for the Stoics made no false promises about the difficult path required to attain such freedom. In this class, we attempt to better understand this ancient School of Philosophy. We will spend most of our time reading the classic works of several Stoics as well those with Stoic sympathies. Interestingly these vanguards could be found in all walks of Roman and Greek life. Epictetus was a slave, Seneca - a Statesman, Cicero -Romeâs greatest orator, and Marcus Eurelious - emperor. In order to understand the Stoics, however, one must understand, at least in outline, their heroâs and their rivals. For the Stoics, there was no greater hero than Socrates. Epictetus implores, âeven though you are not yet a Socrates, you ought to live as someone trying to become a Socrates.â So we will consider the hero of Socrates through Platoâs Euthophro, Apology, and Phaedo. We will also consider the leading rival philosophic theories to the Stoics by reading the Nichomachean Ethics by Aristotle, Outlines of Pyrrhonism by the skeptic philsopher Sextus Empiricus, and On the Nature of Things by the Epicurean philosopher Leviticus. Graded events include a take home mid-term, a take home final, and two papers.

title: CBL:  Philosophy of Education
description: In this course, we will examine perennial, philosophical questions about the nature, purposes, content, control, and methods of education and schooling. These questions include: â¢ What is it to be an educated person? â¢ What should the social functions of education be? â¢ What should children learn and how should have the authority to make this decision? â¢ Do we have obligations to provide equal education to all children? â¢ How does education impact self-identify and how should we respond to this? â¢ How should we respond to segregation and separatism in education? You will also be introduced to ongoing disagreements about specific policies and practices of schooling in the U.S. today Policies and practices to be examined include: â¢ No Child Left Behind â¢ Ability grouping and tracking â¢ Homeschooling â¢ Deschooling â¢ School funding â¢ Sex education â¢ School vouchers You will study your own and your classmates' beliefs about education and schooling, and subject these beliefs to critical evaluation. The course will integrate community-based learning (CBL) experiences that will immerse you in off-campus settings in which activities relevant to education take place (for instance, schools, classrooms, policy meetings, and community organizations). This component of the course will require that you devote a total of 12-14 hours during the semester participating in these activities. These hours must come from activities approved in advance by the professor. The other course assignments reflect a recognition of the time commitment required to complete the community-based learning work. Much of the course will be conducted through discussion and small- and whole-group activities. The primary assignments for the course will consist of short written responses (in the form of blog entries) to the professor's prompts which will require that you reflect on themes from the course readings and engage in an online interactive discussion with your classmates. You will also guide small group discussions about your community-based experiences, and submit short written analyses of the experiences as well.

title: CBL: Civic Engagement and Public Education
description: PHIL/AMST 157 CBL 4 credits: Civic Engagement and Education This course combines reflection and action. In addition to the twice-weekly meetings on campus, students will be expected to invest significant additional time working with high school students at a low-performing public school located in Southeast D.C. The school serves a student population that is 100% African American and predominately from low-income families. GU students will travel to the high school two times a week to lead a group of students as they all work together to identify and address a community-based problem. Georgetown and the high school students will plan and carry out a public event at which they will share the work they have done during the semester. Students should expect to commit roughly 6-8 hours per week traveling to and from the school and working with the high school students. The GU and high school students will also plan and carry out a public event at which they will share their work with the community. In addition to the work at the high school and the conference, the course will include several other out-of-the classroom responsibilities such as hosting a visit by the high school students to the GU campus and planning with GU classmates for the meetings with the high school students. These community-based experiences will be supplemented by readings, class discussions, and blog writing assignments. We will study the role of schooling in preparing and enabling (or failing to prepare and enable) youth to have a voice in their schools and communities. We will focus specifically on the relationships between democracy and schooling; the kinds of knowledge, skills, and character traits needed to be effective in working toward social change through community action; the conditions that must be provided if students are to become active participants in democratic life; and the role of the hidden curriculum in denying students from particular demographic backgrounds access to the kind of learning experiences that enable effective civic and academic engagement. The focus will be primarily on schools and students in Washington, D.C. public schools. All GU students enrolled in the course are expected to take a very active role in the conduct of the course itself and in the work within the high school. However, this course does not require any previous experience working with youth or in schools. While the course is very demanding in several senses, students who enroll report that it is also an incredibly valuable learning experience unlike anything they have experienced through their other, campus-based coursework.

title: Theories of Human Nature
description: Theories of human nature have been offered from many quarters. Religions, the sciences, literature, and philosophy, to select a few prominent examples, have all offered their own insights into what it is to be a human being. In this course we will focus on some of the distinctive contributions made by the philosophical tradition over the course of its long history. In the first instance, we will examine the way philosophy has often given pride of place to rationality and our cognitive abilities; human beings, we are told, are rational animals, thinking things, etc. However, we will also look at aspects of human experience that these accounts sometimes neglect, such as the important place of emotions and sociality in our lives. Another central question of the course centers on the nature of human nature itself: is human nature something static and unchanging or something dynamic and fluid that can evolve over time and place? We will pursue these questions by reading a diverse set of authors including Aristotle, Descartes, Locke, Hume, Marx, Freud, Sartre, and Heidegger. With the help of these authors, we will get a better sense of the richness and complexity of human experience, and perhaps in the process come to a deeper understanding of ourselves.

title: Personal Identity and the Self
description: The central question of this course is what makes you who you are. One way that philosophers have approached this question is to focus on what makes you the same person today that you were yesterday and will be tomorrow. Is it your psychological or bodily features or something beyond these, such as the possession of a soul? However, philosophers have also approached the question by focusing on the features that make you the particular person that you are. This approach asks what makes up your selfâis it what you value, where you come from, or the different groups youâre a part of? In this course, we will explore the question of what makes you who you are from both of these perspectives. In the first part of the course, the general topic will be what makes you the same person over time. We will consider such issues as when your life begins and ends. The second part of the course will be devoted to examining what makes up your identity, or self. A central concern of this part of the course will be to investigate the extent to which your identity is voluntarily chosen. For example, can your relationships to others make you a particular type of person whether you like it or not? Finally, we will connect the question of what makes you who you are to ethics. Particularly, we will explore how who you are affects what you should do. Our approach in this class will be to look at how both significant figures from the history of philosophy and contemporary philosophers deal with these questions. Course requirements include regular in-class participation, two papers, a debate, and a final exam.

title: Skepticism
description: We all think we know things. Here are some of the things we think we know: Penguins can't fly. George Washington was the first President of the United States. I have two hands. But sometimes, we think we know something and then are given reason to doubt that this is the case. In those cases, we usually retract our knowledge claim. If I claim to know that it is cold outside, but then I see people wandering around in shorts, I should probably back down. But what if we have reason to doubt everything, or almost everything? Then it might seem that we don't know anything (or that we know very little). This is the problem of skepticism. In this class, we will see how this problem arises and examine some of the responses that have been offered throughout the history of philosophy. The class is discussion-based; students should come prepared to offer a critical response to the material. Course work will include a take-home test and two papers, with pop quizzes issued if discussion flags.

title: Text Seminar
description: Text Seminar PHL 320.01 Spring 2010 We will dedicate the text seminar to Thomas Aquinasâs classic âThe Treatise on Human Natureâ. Thomas Aquinas was the most influential philosopher of the middle ages, and he had as one of his central projects the understanding of human nature. His point of departure for this were the works of Aristotle, so a close reading of this Treatise will afford students not only with an in-depth look at the detailed and articulated series of discussions by Aquinas, but will also help familiarize students with the thought of Aristotle, specifically his discussion of the soul. This is what Aquinas concentrates on and hence we will be reading an influential medieval examination of topics today found in the philosophy of mind, action theory and epistemology. Specifically we will be reading Aquinasâs Treatise as found in questions seventy-five to eighty-nine of the first part of the Summa Theologiae, in which he concentrates much of his discussion, not on the human body, but on the human soul. In discussing what exactly it is, he does not opt for a Cartesian mind/body dualism, nor is reductionist of mental states to bodily states. Other topics include the relation of the soul to the body, the workings of the sense and intellect, the will and the passions, sensuality, and personal identity. We will be reading the new translation of Robert Pasnau in the Hackett Aquinas, The Treatise of Human Nature: Summa Theologiae 1a, 75-89 Hackett Publishing Company, 2002. I will also recommend getting the study of this material by Robert Pasnau, Thomas Aquinas on Human Nature (Cambridge University Press, 2002. Each student will make a presentations in the course of the semester, and though there will be no exams, there will be one or two papers to submit.

title: CBL: Foundations of Education
description: This course is organized as a group investigation into the following question: Is public education necessary? To address the question, we will first need to spend time figuring out what the question could be taken to mean. Our inquiry will also lead us to examine our most basic assumptions about education and learning, such as the necessity of compulsory formal schooling for young people, and the capacities and rights of people under 18. We'll ask whether schools are good for children or and our society, and whether education should be organized and distributed in the way that it is. Since the specific questions to be raised through the class's inquiry will be determined in part by the students themselves as the semester unfolds, the specific content of the course will also be determined in part by the students. While the specific content is currently indeterminate, the course (in any case) will be a cross-disciplinary examination the ideals and practices of American education and schooling: to address the question that the course raises we will need to delve into the philosophical, historical, sociological, and political disciplinary foundations of education. We will read the original writings of individuals who have influenced the practice and theory of schooling and education in order to gain insight into the historical expectations about the role of public schooling in the United States and to see if those ideas have merit. We will look at public policies as artifacts that represent particular views about the purposes of schooling and education, the nature of the good society and life, and the role of the state. Given the ideals of liberal democracy that permeate our culture and our system of schooling, we will focus much of our attention on the nature of these ideals, their implications for education, and the challenges they present. We will also need to be attentive to the fact that schooling has been and continues to be a means of both empowering and denying power, of transmitting privilege across generations, and providing opportunity. Throughout our inquiry we'll need keep in mind the distinction between facts and norms, between questions about what is and those about what ought to be, and between actual practices and ideals. Since our inquiry relates to things that occur in the world and things that we want to see happen in the world rather than just the realm of ideas, in addition to philosophical argument and analysis, our inquiry will need to be attentive to the world around us. For this reason, this course has been designated a community-based learning (cbl) course; students will be required to commit a total of 12-14 hours during the semester participating in off-campus activities related to the course. The local schools and the many community groups focused on education provide a rich environment for our inquiry, and the hours you spend in the community will provide you with direct experiences in that environment in order to enhance your understanding of education in a way that is simply not possible through readings and classroom based experiences alone. The specific community activities to satisfy the cbl requirements will be selected by the professor. The other course assignments reflect a recognition of the time commitment required to complete the community-based learning work. Much of the course will be conducted through discussion. The primary assignments for the course will consist of short written responses (in the form of blog entries) to the professor's prompts throughout the semester; these prompts will require that you reflect on themes from the course readings and engage in an online interactive discussion with your classmates. You will also guide small group discussions about your community-based experiences, and submit short written analyses of the experiences as well.

title: History of Ancient and Medieval Philosophy
description: BRADLEY: PHIL-386-01: HISTORY OF ANCIENT & MEDIEVAL PHILOSOPHY Spring 2010 This course surveys some of the major themes of ancient Greek and Medieval Philosophy: Knowledge and Opinion, Being and Becoming, God and the First Causes, Cosmos, Soul and Immortality, Reason and Faith. The continuity between the two periods will be stressed. We will read works of Parmenides, Heraclitus, Plato, Aristotle, Plotinus, Proclus, Augustine, Boethius, Anselm, Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, and Aquinas.. Medieval Philosophy was born from the creative interaction of biblical faith with Greek philosophy. The issue, the relationship between philosophy and religious faith, is complicated because among western Christians, medieval philosophy mostly existed in the context of various theologies. But, throughout the course, we will consider the implications of Ãtienne Gilson's remark: "A philosophy may draw inspiration from a revelation and be true, and if true, it is because it is good philosophy" (Spirit of Mediaeval Philosophy [New York: Charles Scribner's Sons, 1940], p. 406). Until the thirteenth century, Neoplatonism (which attempted to unify the Platonic and Aristotelian traditions) provided the dominant philosophical framework for speculative theology. The most illustrious of the Neoplatonists, Plotinus (205-270 A.D.), profoundly influenced Augustine (354-430), especially in regard to the mind's approach to divine reality through interior recollection. Augustine himself dominated subsequent western theology, as can be seen in the writings of Anselm of Canterbury (1033-1109), until, in the late twelfth century, the translation and introduction of Aristotle into the Latin west, often via the Islamic philosophical tradition of the Arabs, provoked a philosophical revolution in regard to the prevailing Augustinianism. But the âChristian Aristotelianismâ of Thomas Aquinas (1225-1274), which marks a new stage in the development of medieval philosophy, was, in fact, a creative synthesis of the Augustinian-Neoplatonic tradition (especially as mediated through the Procline Neoplatonism of Pseudo-Dionysius) with Aristotle. Of special interest is Aquinasâs critique, in the name of Aristotle, of the Aristotelians (the so-called âLatin Averroistsâ) in the Arts Faculty at Paris. title: History of Modern Philosophy
description: Classic philosophical texts of the 16th through the 18th centuries will be examined in the context of major historical developments in the philosophy of the period. The emphasis in lectures will be on textual analysis and the historical development of modern philosophy. The figures of this period traditionally highlighted in scholarly writings are Hobbes, Descartes, Locke, Leibniz, Spinoza, Berkeley, Hume, and Kant. These philosophers will be examined, but the works of less renowned figures will also be examined.

title: The Body and Sexual Difference
description: The body and sexual difference Spring 2010 Wilfried Ver Eecke Descartes argued that the body and the mind are two substances which are so different that the one cannot be thought of as influencing the other, except minimally. Baier defends the thesis that women abide by a different ethic than men. Women live by an ethics of care and men by an ethics of rules. The body, in particular the sexually differentiated body, makes thus, according to Baier, a constitutive contribution to the life of the mind. How can we understand this implicit claim by Baier? In this course we will study, first, how in the history of modern philosophy we notice a slow appreciation of the role of the body for the life of the mind and an even slower appreciation of sexual difference. Second, we will then study how psychoanalytic ideas of Oedipal and pre-Oedipal developments of the child provide a philosophically relevant understanding of the claim by Baier that men and women live by a different ethic and thus in some sense develop and experience a different life of the mind. We will start the course by surveying crucial figures in the history of modern philosophy. We will look at Descartesâ radical separation between the body and the life of the mind. We will see Kantâs positive view of the role of the body for perception but its negative role for ethics. We will discover the centrality of the body for Husserlâs and Merleau-Pontyâs phenomenology. We will study the crucial function of the body and its necessary death for Heideggerâs idea of finitude. We will see the central role of the body in Sartreâs analysis of the look through his analysis of shame and also through his analysis of bad faith. However, these authors do not explicitly address sexual difference. Sartre does have sexual examples for bad faith but does not give sexual difference a crucial or necessary function. De Beauvoir and Merleau-Ponty introduce the function of sexual difference for the life of the mind. Still it is only the beginning. In the second part of the course we will study the insights of psychoanalysis. Both Freud and Lacan point to sexual difference as crucial for the life of the mind. In discovering sexual difference the child must accept not to be everything. It is only boy or girl. In Freud there are two lines of thought. One is misogynic in that he concentrates on the look as the instrument of discovery. Here both genders discover the absence of the penis in girls, hence the idea that women are less than men. The second line of thought in Freud is the idea of the mystery of women and thus Freudâs confession that he did not understand what women want. Lacan stresses the psychoanalytic idea that human infants need the support of their narcissism in order to assume their body but also need the Oedipal discovery and disappointment that they are not everything for the most important other person, the mother. One of Lacanâs novel ideas is that there are theoretically two reactions possible to the experience of the Oedipal disappointment. The first goes in the direction of accepting the disappointment as is often the case with girls. This leads to a greater development of language and interpersonal skills. The second goes in the direction of refusal as is often the case with boys. This leads to a macho attitude promoting aggressive achievements and repression of feelings. Lacan then summarizes these ideas in his thesis about human sexuation. A Lacanian philosopher Collette Soler wrote a book âWhat Lacan said about Women.â We will use Solerâs book and Evansâ âDictionary of Lacanian Psychoanalysisâ for the course. The other material will consist of articles or chapters of books which will be made available through blackboard or the library web-side. Reading material Evans, D. (1996). An Introductory Dictionary of Lacanian Psychoanalysis. London and New York: Routledge. Soler, Colette 2006 What Lacan said about Women. A Psychoanalytic Study. John Holland, trans. New York: Other Press. ISBN # 1-59051-170-0

title: Epistemology
description: Philosophy 406: Epistemology Spring 2010 Epistemology = Theory of Knowledge The course begins with an examination of some hoary old texts from the history of philosophy that serves to set up and make urgent the classic problem of the theory of knowledge: What is it, and how do we get it? What follows is a survey of contemporary approaches to either answering the question or showing that it is predicated on a mistake (either because there's no such thing or because we just can't get it).

title: Philosophy of Physics
description: Philosophy 418: Foundations of Physics (The Quantum Theory) Spring 2010 This is a course in the philosophical foundations of modern physics. The course will be self-contained and so no particular knowledge of physics will be presumed. However a willingness to make use of (and learn if necessary) some small bits of algebra and geometry will be necessary. Here's what the course is about: In the foundations of physics, the distinction between doing physics and doing philosophy is not clear. Partly this course will be an attempt to clarify that distinction. But the bulk of the course will be spent addressing issues that fall just on the philosophy side of the line. Our primary concern this term will be with the QUANTUM THEORY. This theory is the fundamental underpinning of the best tested, most accurate, apparently comprehensive theory of nature, the Standard Model of particle physics. But the conceptual foundations of the theory are shrouded in mystery and have received no satisfactory interpretation---and we've been looking for well over a century. The theory gives rise to the following questions: Is it really true that the moon isn't there when I'm not looking? Is there really no causality world? Are all events connected by some kind of "spooky action at a distance"? We will consider these, and other questions in an effort either to settle the foundational question or to show that attempts to settle the question are simply misguided and that we should educate our intuitions rather than attempting to get the theory to conform to them.

title: Euthanasia in Nazi Germany
description: Instructor: LeRoy Walters EUTHANASIA IN NAZI GERMANY PHIL-422-01 PHIL-522-01 Goal This course will explore how approximately 300,000 people with disabilities were murdered in a program that deceptively claimed to provide its victims with a âgood death.â Overview This course will focus primarily on the years 1939 to 1945 in Germany, Austria, and other parts of Europe annexed or occupied by Germany when Adolf Hitler was in power. We will examine how the program to kill off âunproductiveâ institutionalized adults â and children with physical or mental disabilities â was planned and implemented. We will also seek to discover opponents of the âeuthanasiaâ program and will consider how and how effectively they expressed their opposition. In an attempt to understand the precedents for National-Socialist âeuthanasia,â we will briefly review eugenic sterilization programs in the United States (beginning in 1907) and Germany (beginning in 1934), as well as the plight of German asylum patients after World War I. Visitors to the class will include a psychiatrist, a parent of a child with disabilities, and a historian who has studied National Socialist âeuthanasia.â General Requirements Primary- and secondary-source readings; periodic journals; a field trip or interview; a biographical presentation based on the life of a perpetrator, opponent, or victim of âeuthanasiaâ; and a final term paper. There is no final examination. Textbooks Michael Burleigh, Death and Deliverance: âEuthanasiaâ in Germany 1900-1945 (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1994). ISBN 0-521-47769-7. Henry Friedlander, The Origins of Nazi Genocide: From Euthanasia to the Final Solution (Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1995). ISBN 0-8078-4675-9. Hugh G. Gallagher, By Trust Betrayed: Patients, Physicians, and the License to Kill in the Third Reich (revised edition: Arlington, VA: Vandamere Press, 1995). ISBN 0-918339-36-7 J. Noakes and G. Pridham, eds., Nazism 1919-1945, Volume 3: Foreign Policy, War and Racial Extermination: A Documentary Reader (revised edition; Exeter: University of Exeter Press, 2001). ISBN 0-85989-602-1.

title: Topics in Philosophical Logic
description: Course Goals & Description The first part of this course will be an introduction to alethic modal logic, the principles of reasoning involving necessity, possibility, contingency and related notions. This part will consist of lectures and weekly problems sets. The second part of the course will be a seminar on recent work in various areas of philosophical logic. The topics covered will depend on the interests of students and professor, but may include the following: generalized quantifiers (âfew,â âmost,â and the like) non-monotonic logics (in which adding assumptions to a valid argument may render it invalid), epistemic and doxastic logics (i.e., logics of knowledge and belief), deontic logics (logics of permission and obligation), substructural logics (i.e., non-classical logics in which the rules for connectives are classical but the structural rules are altered). In the second part of the course, all students will be expected to read, and to be prepared to discuss, the assigned material. Students will take turns leading discussion. Students registered under the graduate number will be required to do some additional work.

title: Absurdity, Irony, and Philosophy
description: Phil 445 Absurdity, Irony, Philosophy "At any streetcorner the feeling of absurdity can strike any man in the face" (Albert Camus). What is this feeling of absurdity, and what is its philosophical significance? In this course, we will investigate the absurd experience and what it shows us about human nature, the nature of philosophical thought, and how we should live. Pursuing threads opened up by Camus' and Thomas Nagel's analyses of the absurd feeling, we will take an eclectic, thematic road trip through contemporary and historical thought. In the first part of the course, we will contrast Camus and Nagel on the absurd experience and how we live with it. For Camus, the experience of absurdity urgently raises the question of how - and whether - to go on living. For Nagel, the absurd experience is closely related to philosophical skepticism, after which (according to both Nagel and Hume) we can unproblematically continue with our ordinary lives - albeit ironically. But what does this look like? In the second part of the course, we consider what irony is and what it might be to live ironically. Rorty, Kierkegaard and Hadot shed light on this - the latter two through appeals to Socratic irony and Socrates' way of life, which we will explore by reading parts of Plato's Symposium. In the final part of the course, we shall consider whether the absurd experience calls for something more radical - a post-skeptical reconfiguration of our relationship to the everyday and our manner of philosophising, as outlined by Cavell and illustrated in comedies of remarriage like The Philadelphia Story. In reading these authors on absurdity, irony and philosophy, our goal is to understand some of the ways in which our everyday intimacy with the world and our lives can break down, the role of philosophical reflection in this breakdown, and what difference (if any) this can or should make to how we lead our lives.

title: Metaphysics
description: In contemporary philosophy the term âmetaphysicsâ means the philosophical inquiry into the nature or reality of what seem to be the most pervasive features of both reality in general and of ourselves, and into questions of ultimate explanation. This course will take up a selection of issues in metaphysics as they have been treated in Anglo-American philosophy (with some incursions from the Antipodes) in the 20th and 21st centuries. In particular, we will be concerned with whether what seem to be certain pervasive features of reality really are as they seem, and if they are not, what we should take reality really to be like. We shall start with the idea of existence itself and the notion of possibility. We shall then consider: the apparent commitment we have to there being universal or common features present at more than one place at a given time (as when we say that this piece of paper here has the very same color as that one over there); time and its apparent flow and the reality of the past and future; causation, whereby one item seems to bring about or make another happen, both in the natural world and also in our voluntary actions; and the nature of consciousness and our conception of ourselves as comprised of what seen to be two very different sorts of things: a mind and a body.

title: Moral Theory of Aquinas
description: TBA

title: Kant's Critique
description: Philosophy 501: Kantâs Critique of Pure Reason Prof. W. Blattner In this course we shall pursue a close reading of as much of Kant's Critique of Pure Reason as possible, consistent with reading it carefully and critically. The Critique is one of those rare texts that thoroughly and systematically reconfigured the philosophical landscape. The Critique was the tipping point between early modern Rationalism and Empiricism, on the one hand, and on the other hand, the "wild years" of Romanticism, German Idealism, Hegel, and nineteenth century European philosophy (as Safranski has called them). Kant reconceived most of the inherited problems of metaphysics and epistemology: realism v idealism; the nature of substance, causality, and the natural world; the constitution of mind and the mind-body problem; the problem of free will and determinism; skepticism and the foundation of human knowledge. Kant effects his philosophical revolution by means of a novel account of the human power of representation. He replaces the early modern Theory of Ideas inaugurated by Descartes with a radically new transcendental analysis of the mind's power to represent an objective world by deploying concepts to work up the data of the senses. We will aim to come to terms with both Kant's "Copernican revolution" in philosophy (as he called it) and the details of his argumentation. To do this, I will begin each new topic by lecturing, which I expect to drift into discussion. Student interests will dictate the pace and precise focus of the course, to the extent consistent with not becoming bogged down early in the text and missing some of the juicy bits later. Students will write a single approx. 20 page research paper for this course.

title: Euthanasia in Nazi Germany
description: Instructor: LeRoy Walters EUTHANASIA IN NAZI GERMANY PHIL-422-01 PHIL-522-01 Goal This course will explore how approximately 300,000 people with disabilities were murdered in a program that deceptively claimed to provide its victims with a âgood death.â Overview This course will focus primarily on the years 1939 to 1945 in Germany, Austria, and other parts of Europe annexed or occupied by Germany when Adolf Hitler was in power. We will examine how the program to kill off âunproductiveâ institutionalized adults â and children with physical or mental disabilities â was planned and implemented. We will also seek to discover opponents of the âeuthanasiaâ program and will consider how and how effectively they expressed their opposition. In an attempt to understand the precedents for National-Socialist âeuthanasia,â we will briefly review eugenic sterilization programs in the United States (beginning in 1907) and Germany (beginning in 1934), as well as the plight of German asylum patients after World War I. Visitors to the class will include a psychiatrist, a parent of a child with disabilities, and a historian who has studied National Socialist âeuthanasia.â General Requirements Primary- and secondary-source readings; periodic journals; a field trip or interview; a biographical presentation based on the life of a perpetrator, opponent, or victim of âeuthanasiaâ; and a final term paper. There is no final examination. Textbooks Michael Burleigh, Death and Deliverance: âEuthanasiaâ in Germany 1900-1945 (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1994). ISBN 0-521-47769-7. Henry Friedlander, The Origins of Nazi Genocide: From Euthanasia to the Final Solution (Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1995). ISBN 0-8078-4675-9. Hugh G. Gallagher, By Trust Betrayed: Patients, Physicians, and the License to Kill in the Third Reich (revised edition: Arlington, VA: Vandamere Press, 1995). ISBN 0-918339-36-7 J. Noakes and G. Pridham, eds., Nazism 1919-1945, Volume 3: Foreign Policy, War and Racial Extermination: A Documentary Reader (revised edition; Exeter: University of Exeter Press, 2001). ISBN 0-85989-602-1.

title: Seminar: Philosophy of Language
